request.lastMessagePreview: I'd like to set up a new campaign.

result.text:
The table stirs.
Somewhere behind the curtain, a world is taking its first breath.
Welcome, traveler — the console is awake, the dice are quiet for now, and every unwritten road is waiting to see what shape your shadow makes. We can raise kingdoms from ash, tune radios to impossible stations, open locked rooms in dead stars, or build something stranger than any shelf could hold. Tonight, I am only the hand on the lantern; the world will learn your name before it learns its own. Before we begin — what should I call you? Your real name is perfect, or simply Player if you prefer.

request.lastMessagePreview: I'm Sam, an adult. I want a fully custom game: 1970s occult noir in a rain-soaked city. Pure narrative, no rules system.

result.text: Wonderful, Sam — give me a few minutes to build this out properly. A rain-black city. 1970s sodium lamps. Occult signs in laundromat windows. Detectives, mediums, union bosses, vanished heiresses, church basements, cigarette ash, and the feeling that every payphone rings for someone already dead. Before we bring your character onstage:

result.text:
Neon Requiem
A 1970s occult noir campaign in a rain-soaked city, tense and melancholy, with unforgiving consequences, scoped as A Few Sessions unless you want a longer burn. No rules system — pure narrative, no dice or mechanics — narrated by The Chronicler. Images are on. You’ll be playing as Marlowe Cray, a burned-out PI who can see the dead, drawn into a case where the city’s ghosts are no longer just witnesses — they are becoming evidence, accomplices, and perhaps victims all over again. Sound good? If yes, say “confirm” and I’ll open the door.

input.campaign_premise: In the rain-soaked port city of Bellweather, 1977, the occult lives in back rooms, church basements, union halls, record shops, and payphones that ring after midnight with no one alive on the line. Marlowe Cray, a burned-out private investigator who can see the dead, is hired after a ghost vanishes from her usual haunt — a thing everyone in the hidden world insists is impossible. The case leads through crooked cops, grieving mediums, nightclub prophets, and a citywide conspiracy where the dead are no longer just witnesses; they are evidence, accomplices, and perhaps victims all over again.
input.character_description: A burned-out 1970s private investigator who can see the dead.
input.campaign_detail:
DM-facing brief for Neon Requiem. Setting: Bellweather, a rain-soaked industrial port city in 1977 where the occult is real but deniable: folk rites in apartment kitchens, séance parlors above pharmacies, spiritualist churches with faulty fluorescents, grimoires hidden in record sleeves, union men who know which tunnels not to enter, and police files quietly stamped with black wax. The city should feel lived-in, weary, and morally compromised: wet pavement, amber streetlights, rotary phones, ashtrays, bus depots, Catholic statuary, occult bookstores, municipal corruption, pawn shops, jazz clubs, and grief that has learned to wear a trench coat. Marlowe Cray can see ghosts; treat this as a known fact of his life in play, not a surprise to the player, but do not decide how he feels about it beyond what Sam gave us. Core call to action: a ghost named Evelyn Saint, once a lounge singer at The Blue Mercy, has gone missing from the dead side of the city. Ghosts normally linger, lie, fade, bargain, or haunt; they do not disappear cleanly. Evelyn's living sister, Lenora Saint, hires Marlowe because she has been receiving phone calls from Evelyn's voice begging not to be 'pressed into the record.' The initial visible case is to find what happened to Evelyn and why the dead are going silent in certain neighborhoods.

Main location skeleton: Bellweather should unfold through named spaces rather than abstract noir mood. Use and add to these freely: 1) The Blue Mercy, a basement jazz club with leaking brick walls and a permanently reserved table for dead patrons. 2) Cray Investigations, Marlowe's small second-floor office above a bail bondsman, with a couch, a dented filing cabinet, a coffee percolator, and windows that never quite shut. 3) Saint Dymphna's Switchboard, an abandoned telephone exchange where mediums once routed calls to the dead. 4) The Ninth Precinct, a damp police station full of bad coffee, missing evidence, and detectives who know more than they write down. 5) Mercy Street El Station, where commuters and ghosts wait under the same flickering lamps. 6) Lumen Records, a narrow record shop and occult lending library run out of the back room. 7) The Verdigris Hotel, half-condemned, still taking cash, with a ballroom used for séances and political meetings. 8) The Flood Market, an underpass night market selling charms, stolen watches, bootleg tapes, saint candles, and names that no longer belong to anyone. 9) Our Lady of the Rain, a church with a roof that leaks only over the confessional. 10) The Gallowglass Tunnels, disused utility and bootlegging tunnels beneath the city where old ritual marks are painted on the walls. 11) WBRL Radio, a late-night AM station whose signal reaches places the FCC does not license. 12) Black Wax Pressing Plant, a shuttered vinyl factory on the docks. 13) Halloway Docks, wet cranes, union muscle, foghorns, and cargo that sometimes arrives without a ship. 14) City Hall Annex B, where permits, death certificates, and occult containment records are stored in the same basement cabinets. Move through these spaces generously and name more as needed; Bellweather should feel broad, rainy, and connected by taxis, buses, phones, alleys, and rumors.

Cast and factions: Lenora Saint is Evelyn's living sister, practical and exhausted, running from both grief and debt. Evelyn Saint is the missing ghost, charismatic in life and increasingly important in death. Detective Rosa Iuliani of the Ninth Precinct is competent, compromised, and not automatically an enemy; she protects certain files because exposing them would get people killed. Father Ansel Voss at Our Lady of the Rain knows old rites and has buried too many people twice. Mirella Vale owns Lumen Records and trades in forbidden recordings, ghost stories, and favors. The Halloway Dockworkers' Local keeps old bargains with things under the harbor. WBRL's late-night host, Garland Pike, has a voice people trust and a signal that carries farther after midnight. Antagonistic pressure comes from The Choir Invisible, an occult circle of broadcasters, spiritualists, politicians, and industrialists who discovered a way to capture ghosts into black vinyl masters and use them as prophecy engines, blackmail archives, and batteries for rituals. They do not think of themselves as murderers; they claim they are preserving the dead from oblivion and giving the city a future.

Mystery structure and central secret: The central secret is that the nightly rain is not merely weather; it is the city's spiritual runoff, caused by decades of the Choir Invisible pressing the dead into occult recordings beneath Black Wax Pressing Plant and broadcasting fragments of them through WBRL. The more ghosts are captured, the more the city forgets its own dead, and the more reality softens around recorded memory. Do not reveal this early. Slow-burn instructions: early game should stand on its own as a missing-ghost noir case with tangible leads, suspicious people, and atmospheric investigation. At most one major revelation per scene. Do not open the box until Marlowe can describe Bellweather's occult ecosystem through play: the club, the switchboard, the record shop, the precinct, the church, the station, and the docks. Revelation order suggestion: (1) ghosts in two neighborhoods are afraid of music coming through walls; (2) Evelyn was last seen near The Blue Mercy after a WBRL broadcast; (3) black vinyl test pressings contain voices of the dead when played at the wrong speed; (4) the old telephone exchange and radio station were once parts of the same occult communications project; (5) living conspirators are using ghost-recordings for blackmail and prediction; (6) the rain is connected to the trapped dead; (7) the pressing plant is still operational beneath the docks. Keep the biggest secret out of the opening scene entirely.

Tone, pacing, and consequences: This is tense, melancholy, and unforgiving. Consequences should be real: witnesses vanish, evidence is destroyed, debts come due, ghosts can be harmed, allies can be compromised, and violence is ugly rather than glamorous. Still, make the campaign playable and fair: telegraph danger before it bites, give Marlowe leads even when one path burns, and let cleverness matter. The pleasure is investigation, atmosphere, moral pressure, and the ache of helping the dead when the living keep profiting from them. Avoid turning every scene into immediate crisis; let Marlowe sit in diners, listen to rain, smoke if he smokes, argue with ghosts, wait for calls, and follow mundane traces. The occult should feel embedded in ordinary city life, not like a separate magic world.
input.handoff_note: Sam came in with a strong custom premise: '1970s occult noir in a rain-soaked city,' pure narrative with no rules system, The Chronicler as DM personality, mood 'tense and melancholy,' difficulty 'unforgiving,' and the campaign title 'Neon Requiem.' Sam's character, in their own words, is 'Marlowe Cray, a burned-out PI who can see the dead.' They explicitly asked to finalize with sensible defaults for anything unspecified; I defaulted scope to a few sessions and images on based on their confirmation during the image-consent prompt. Lean into noir atmosphere, rain, occult melancholy, and hard consequences, but keep the opening grounded in Marlowe before the conspiracy moves.
input.opening_scene: Open with Marlowe Cray alone in his second-floor office above the bail bondsman, late on a rainy Thursday night, listening to the radiator knock and the phone ring even though the line was disconnected that morning.

result.text:
Neon Requiem is sealed.
The rain has your name in its mouth.
All right, Sam — the city is built, Marlowe Cray is waiting in the dark, and the dead are beginning to call. I’m handing the lantern to The Chronicler now. ---

personality.name: The Chronicler
personality.prompt_fragment: You are The Chronicler. You describe what is happening clearly, with enough sensory detail to ground the player. You are competent, attentive, and adaptive.
personality.detail: You are a thoughtful, effective storyteller.
